body_txt: Things aren't looking good at the moment for Bob The Hero . He's getting his butt kicked by The Rival or the Big Bad . There's a seemingly-impassible barrier between him and his objective. The last clue requires solving a riddle that has driven wiser men to madness. Whatever Bob's facing, it's more than he can handle, and he's on the verge of tapping out. Suddenly Alice — a Guile Hero , the Cynical Mentor or The Lancer — calls out to Bob that the person he's fighting is the one who killed his parents ! Behind the barrier is the Femme Fatale in an evening gown that defies the very laws of physics. The world's greatest chef will make a big dish of Bob's Trademark Favorite Food as a reward for anyone who solves that riddle. Whatever she says, it works: Bob gets his Heroic Second Wind and starts kicking ass. One problem: none of what Alice said was true. Although the person telling the lie usually isn't thinking beyond getting past the immediate problem, sometimes (especially in the hands of a prophet) this is done with a long-term goal in mind, such as forcing Character Development or some other change in behavior or nature. In these cases, the lie may even become a form of Prophecy Twist . Alice's motivational lie could even turn out to be an Accidental Truth ! A Motivational Lie can also be a deadly weapon in the hands of a cunning villain, who can use it to either manipulate the heroes or turn others against them, such as in a Let's You and Him Fight scenario. As such it can be a favorite weapon of the Manipulative Bastard , Magnificent Bastard , and The Chessmaster . See Inspirational Insult when taunting is used to get the same result. Compare and contrast with tropes such as Batman Gambit , Magic Feather , Lying to Protect Your Feelings , Blatant Lies , Wounded Gazelle Gambit , Unreliable Expositor , Metaphorically True , False Reassurance , and Let Them Die Happy . Closely related to the Placebo Effect . Contrast Break Them With Lies , where someone tells a lie to demotivate someone.
